hints.paradiso: 'Paradiso' is a noun meaning 'paradise' in the ablative

hints.to go: 'To go' is 'ire' — 'eris' comes from 'sum, esse' (to be)

steps.reveal: eris = you will be (2nd sing. future)

hints.present indicative: Present would be 'es' — 'eris' with -ri- is future
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'sis' — 'eris' is future indicative

hints.eo, ire, ii, itum: These are the parts of 'eo' (to go)
hints.edo, esse, edi, esum: These are the parts of 'edo' (to eat)
hints.erro, errare, erravi, erratum: These are the parts of 'erro' (to wander/err)

hints.accusative of 'ego': Accusative of 'ego' is 'me', but 'mecum' includes the enclitic preposition 'cum'
hints.dative of 'ego': Dative of 'ego' is 'mihi' — 'mecum' is ablative with enclitic 'cum'
hints.genitive of 'ego': Genitive of 'ego' is 'mei' — 'mecum' is ablative with enclitic 'cum'

hints.dative: Dative 'paradiso' looks the same, but 'in' + ablative = 'in' (location)
hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'paradisus' — 'paradiso' is ablative after 'in'
hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'paradisum' — 'in' + accusative means 'into', but here it is location (ablative)

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'paradisi' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'paradisi' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -us — 'paradisi' ends in -i

steps.prompt: Does 'in' + ablative or 'in' + accusative indicate location?
steps.reveal: in paradiso (abl.) = in paradise (location, not motion)

hints.'in' + accusative = location; 'in' + abl: This is backwards — 'in' + ablative = location, 'in' + accusative = motion toward
hints.'in' always takes ablative: 'In' can take either case — ablative for location, accusative for motion toward
hints.'in' always takes accusative: 'In' can take either case — the case determines the meaning

hints.noun: 'Hodie' is indeclinable — it is an adverb, not a noun
hints.adjective: An adjective modifies a noun — 'Hodie' modifies the verb 'eris' as a time adverb
hints.conjunction: A conjunction joins clauses — 'Hodie' indicates when, modifying 'eris'

steps.prompt: What is the theological significance of 'Hodie' placed first?
steps.target: emphatic position stresses the immediacy: 'today' (not in the distant future)
choices: emphatic position stresses the immediacy: 'today' (not in the distant future)
it is merely conventional word order

hints.it is merely conventional word order: Latin word order is flexible — placing 'Hodie' first gives it rhetorical emphasis
hints.it introduces a temporal clause: 'Hodie' is an adverb modifying 'eris', not introducing a subordinate clause
hints.it is a vocative address: 'Hodie' is an adverb meaning 'today', not a form of address

translation: Today you will be with me in paradise
